title: Effective Computations for Weakly Special Loci
abstract:
In this talk, I will discuss some effective computations for variations of integral Hodge structures.
Several years ago, with Ren and Javanpeykar-Kühne, I conjectured (in the Shimura setting) that a variation has only finitely many "non-factor" special subvarieties of degree at most d. Urbanik established this conjecture and, in fact, gave an explicit algorithm.  More recently, Klingler-Otwinowska-Urbanik showed that, when the variation is defined over the algebraic numbers, so too are the non-factor special subvarieties.
In work in progress with Binyamini and Lerer, we show that there is an effectively computable polynomial p such that any non-factor special subvariety of degree d is defined by polynomials of complexity at most p(d) — complexity is the maximum of the degree, the log-height of the coefficients, and the degree of the field of definition. Using this result, we give a separation bound for non-factor special subvarieties in the period domain, also of polynomial strength, thus nearly resolving a conjecture of Lairez-Sertoz.

proof_stages.label: Hodge-theoretic setup and nonfactor weakly specials
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes a smooth quasi-projective variety with a polarized integral variation of Hodge structure and period map, defines weakly special subvarieties via Mumford-Tate groups and normal subgroups, and clarifies the factor/nonfactor and Qbar hypotheses.

proof_stages.label: Degree as an effective complexity for nonfactor weakly specials
proof_stages.summary: Motivated by Zilber-Pink/Pila-Zannier counting and existing finiteness/algorithmic results, the main height-complexity theorem asserts that every degree d nonfactor weakly special subvariety is defined by Qbar polynomials of effectively polynomially bounded complexity.

proof_stages.label: Effective separation statement
proof_stages.summary: A second theorem states that, for Hodge-generic algebraic data, the distance in the compact dual/period-domain geometry from the period image to the corresponding weak Mumford-Tate domain is bounded below by the reciprocal of an effectively computable polynomial in degree, height, and field-degree invariants.

proof_stages.label: Period-torsor reformulation of weakly special loci
proof_stages.summary: Using monodromy, the algebraic monodromy group, and the standard principal G-bundle with its canonical foliation and maps to S and the period domain, the weakly special problem is converted into an algebraic foliated incidence problem whose components project to the desired nonfactor weakly special subvarieties.

proof_stages.label: Abstract foliated incidence theorem
proof_stages.summary: In an axiomatic setting of a foliated algebraic variety P, a subvariety X, and a map to S, the locus of points whose leaf-intersection projects into a k-dimensional degree-d subvariety is defined and asserted to have effectively computable equations of polynomially bounded complexity.

proof_stages.label: Linear-jet proof of polynomial complexity
proof_stages.summary: After reductions, especially to the hypersurface case, the incidence condition is encoded by the nontrivial kernel of a linear map from degree-d polynomials to finite leaf-jets/global functions, so determinant minors give polynomial rather than exponential complexity bounds.

proof_stages.label: Application to weakly special equations and period torsors
proof_stages.summary: Applying the abstract foliated theorem to the period-torsor setup proves the main polynomial height-complexity bound and also yields polynomially bounded equations for associated algebraic monodromy groups and standard principal subbundles.

proof_stages.label: Transfer of algebraic separation to period-domain separation
proof_stages.summary: Liouville-type bounds first give separation on the algebraic variety S, and a leaf-distance estimate together with a computable excess-intersection locus Sigma, controlled by weakly atypical/Hodge loci and avoided by Hodge-generic points, transfers the polynomial lower bound to the period domain.

examples.name: Y(1)^2 and modular-polynomial curves
examples.note: In Y(1)^2 the factor weakly special subvarieties are points and horizontal or vertical lines, while the nonfactor ones are modular-polynomial curves; Cohen's height estimates and Liouville bounds illustrate the polynomial complexity and separation statements rather than supply the proof.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker identifies weakly special subvarieties in Y(1)^2, separates factor lines/points from modular-polynomial curves, uses Cohen’s height asymptotic for Φ_n, and derives polynomial-style height and separation bounds for them.

passes.reason: Y(1)^2 with modular-polynomial/Hecke curves is a standard infinite-family Shimura example used to illustrate the theorem and height bounds, not to refute a claim.
The Hecke/modular-polynomial curves in the standard surface Y(1)^2 form a systematic weakly-special family used to illustrate the height theorem, not to refute a claim.
The modular-polynomial curves in Y(1)^2 are used as a standard Hecke/modular illustration of the height bounds, not as a sporadic, degenerate, extremal, or adversarial case.
Y(1)^2 and the modular-polynomial curves are standard members of an infinite Shimura/Hecke family used as a model case for the height theorem, not as a failure case.
Standard Hecke/modular-polynomial curves in the modular surface form an infinite illustrative family, not a sporadic, limiting, or extremal case, and they are not used to refute a claim.
Standard modular-polynomial/Hecke curves in Y(1)^2 form a systematic infinite family and are used to illustrate height bounds, not to refute a claim.
A standard infinite family of Hecke/modular-polynomial weakly special curves in the Shimura surface Y(1)^2, used as an illustrative benchmark for the height bound rather than to refute a claim.
Used as a benchmark illustration of nonfactor weakly special Hecke curves in the symmetric Shimura surface Y(1)^2, not to refute a claim.
used as an illustrative Shimura/Hecke family of nonfactor weakly special curves with known height bounds, not to refute a claim
It is used as a standard Shimura/Hecke illustration of the height theorem, and the modular-polynomial curves in Y(1)^2 are locally symmetric weakly special correspondences.

The modular-polynomial curves are Hecke/weakly special curves in the locally symmetric Shimura surface Y(1)^2, and Cohen’s asymptotic makes them a sharp height–degree benchmark rather than a refutation.

Used as a standard illustrative Shimura example of nonfactor weakly special Hecke/modular-polynomial curves in Y(1)^2, not to refute a claim and not sporadic, collapsed, extremal, or maximally symmetric in the required sense.

examples.name: Noether-Lefschetz loci for quartic K3 surfaces
examples.note: The loci NL_delta in the parameter space of smooth quartic surfaces are presented as a key inspiration: known exponential height bounds and conjectural degree^(1+epsilon) bounds motivate the theorem's polynomial height target and its period-separation application.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker sets up the quartic K3 parameter space, defines Noether–Lefschetz strata via Picard rank and the explicit intersection discriminant δ(γ), and applies the height-bound theorem to compare exponential versus polynomial bounds.

passes.reason: Noether-Lefschetz loci for quartic K3 surfaces are standard Hodge loci with Picard-rank jumping in a systematic family, used as a motivating test case for height bounds rather than as a counterexample.
Noether-Lefschetz loci for quartic K3 surfaces are standard discriminant-indexed Hodge loci with Picard-rank jump, used as a motivating comparison for height bounds rather than as a counterexample.
Noether-Lefschetz loci for quartic K3 surfaces are presented as a motivating Hodge-locus family for height estimates, not as a counterexample or a structurally exceptional/extreme case.
Noether-Lefschetz loci for quartic K3 surfaces are systematic Hodge-special divisors with jumping Picard rank, used as an application/comparison for height bounds rather than as counterexamples.
Noether-Lefschetz divisors/loci for quartic K3 surfaces are standard special-jump loci used to illustrate height bounds, not exceptional classification outliers or counterexamples.
Noether-Lefschetz loci for quartic K3 surfaces are standard discriminant-indexed Hodge loci used as a motivating comparison for bounds, not as failures of a statement.
Noether-Lefschetz loci form a systematic Hodge-theoretic family of Picard-rank-jump subvarieties for quartic K3 surfaces, used as a motivating application of the polynomial height bounds.
Used as a motivating height-bound example; Noether-Lefschetz divisors form a systematic locally symmetric Hodge-locus family, not a sporadic or failure case.
used as an illustrative Hodge/Noether-Lefschetz family for quartic K3s, an infinite Picard-jump locus rather than a sporadic, degenerate, or extremal obstruction
It is used as a motivating K3 Hodge-locus application, and Noether-Lefschetz divisors are orthogonal locally symmetric special subloci rather than sporadic, extremal, or degenerate objects.
Noether-Lefschetz loci for quartic K3s are systematic Heegner/Hodge loci in an orthogonal locally symmetric K3 period space, used as an application of the bounds.

Used as an illustrative/inspirational Hodge-locus example for the height bounds; Noether-Lefschetz divisors form a systematic family indexed by discriminant and are not counterexamples or structurally exceptional/degenerate/extremal/symmetric.
